executiveSummary.text: AIG is a complex, restructuring multi-line insurer that has made meaningful progress simplifying its operations and strengthening its balance sheet, but the valuation at low-14x earnings is not compelling given persistent catastrophe volatility and execution uncertainty, warranting a REDUCE.

businessModel.text: AIG is one of the largest insurance organisations in the world, operating through two core divisions following its multi-year restructuring: General Insurance (Commercial & Personal Lines) ~65% of pre-tax income; commercial P&C, specialty lines, A&H, personal auto and homeowners Life & Retirement ~35% of pre-tax income; individual life, group benefits, retirement accounts AIG's commercial insurance operations serve large multinational corporations, middle-market businesses, and specialty niche markets including political risk, cyber liability, and marine insurance. The personal lines business has been significantly scaled back following the 2021 Strawberry Hill reinsurance transaction. AIG earns revenues primarily through insurance premiums and investment income on the float generated by its insurance liabilities.

recentCatalysts.text: Q4 2025 Earnings In Line: AIG reported Q4 2025 adjusted EPS of $1.35, in line with consensus, with general insurance combined ratios improving modestly to 97.8%. Full-year results were broadly stable but not transformative. Active Capital Management: AIG repurchased approximately $1.2 billion of its own shares in Q4 2025, demonstrating commitment to returning capital to shareholders as the restructuring generates free cash flow; buybacks continue into Q1 2026. Rate Firming in Commercial Lines: The commercial P&C market continues to see positive rate momentum in 2026, particularly in specialty lines and excess casualty, supporting earnings in the core General Insurance division. CEO Succession Uncertainty: Speculation around CEO Peter Zaffino's succession timeline has created overhang on the shares; no formal announcement had been made as of April 2026, leaving investors uncertain about the strategic direction post-restructuring.

thesisEvaluation.text: Scenario Score Probability Commentary Bull 66 30% Combined ratio improves to sub-95; catastrophe losses normalise; Life & Retirement delivers steady earnings; buybacks accelerate; EPS reaches $7.00-7.50; shares re-rate to 16-17x, reaching $100+. Base 57 50% Stable underwriting; modest Life & Retirement growth; CAT losses remain elevated but manageable; buybacks support EPS; shares drift to $80-85 on mid-teens P/E. Bear 40 20% Major CAT event (hurricane, earthquake) causes large losses; reserve deficiencies resurface; Life & Retirement interest margin compressed by rate environment; shares fall to $50-58. Conviction Score: 66x0.30 + 57x0.50 + 40x0.20 = 56.3 The bear-case downside exceeds the bull-case upside at the current valuation, supporting a REDUCE rating.

keyRisks.text: # Risk Description 1 Catastrophe loss volatility AIG retains meaningful catastrophe exposure in its General Insurance book. A major US hurricane or earthquake event in a single quarter could wipe out annual earnings and trigger a sharp share price decline. 2 Reserve adequacy AIG has a history of reserve deficiencies, most notably from the 2000s ALFS business. Any re-emergence of reserve shortfalls would undermine investor confidence and trigger a sell-off disproportionate to the actual loss. 3 Leadership transition CEO Zaffino has been the architect of the restructuring; an unplanned or poorly managed succession could signal a pause in strategic execution or a change in capital allocation priorities. 4 Life & Retirement spread compression AIG's Life & Retirement segment is sensitive to interest rate movements; if the Federal Reserve cuts rates faster than expected, the spread income on fixed annuity products compresses, reducing earnings. 5 Structural complexity AIG's multi-segment, multi-geography structure creates opacity for investors; the holding company complexity and legacy litigation overhang (AIGFP / Financial Products unit) still generate occasional surprise charges.

risks.risk: Catastrophe loss volatility - AIG retains meaningful catastrophe exposure in its General Insurance book. A major US hurricane or earthquake event in a single quarter could wipe out annual earnings and trigger a sharp share price decline.

Reserve adequacy - AIG has a history of reserve deficiencies, most notably from the 2000s ALFS business. Any re-emergence of reserve shortfalls would undermine investor confidence and trigger a sell-off disproportionate to the actual loss.

Leadership transition - CEO Zaffino has been the architect of the restructuring; an unplanned or poorly managed succession could signal a pause in strategic execution or a change in capital allocation priorities.

Life & Retirement spread compression - AIG's Life & Retirement segment is sensitive to interest rate movements; if the Federal Reserve cuts rates faster than expected, the spread income on fixed annuity products compresses, reducing earnings.

Structural complexity - AIG's multi-segment, multi-geography structure creates opacity for investors; the holding company complexity and legacy litigation overhang (AIGFP / Financial Products unit) still generate occasional surprise charges.

whoShouldOwn.text:
Own:
• Deep-value insurance investors attracted to the sub-15x P/E and active buyback programme
• Those with high conviction in the commercial lines pricing cycle who want diversified insurer exposure

Avoid:
• Quality-focused investors who prefer cleaner, simpler P&C franchises (e.g. PGR, TRV) with better combined ratio track records
• Investors who require earnings visibility and dislike CAT event risk that can swing quarterly results by 20-30%
• Growth-oriented investors; AIG is a restructuring story, not a compounding story

recommendation.text: Rating: REDUCE AIG has made genuine progress simplifying its business and returning capital to shareholders, and the buyback programme is meaningful at current prices. However, the shares trade near the middle of their 52-week range with a P/E of 14x that leaves little margin of safety. A large catastrophe event or reserve charge could push the shares materially lower, and there are cleaner insurance franchise names available at comparable valuations. Reduce and await a better entry point. Framework Detail Entry point $76.87 (current); wait for pullback to $68-70 to initiate new positions Target price (12 months) $82-$87 on 14-15x base case EPS of $5.75-$6.00 (10-15% upside) Stop-loss $67.00 (below the 52-week low; would signal a fundamental deterioration in underwriting or balance sheet confidence) Position sizing Reduce; avoid initiating new positions at current levels; preferred P&C alternatives offer better risk/reward Key trigger to upgrade Sub-95 combined ratio achieved; clarity on CEO succession; reserve review completed with no deficiencies; pullback to $68-70

entryExit.text: Entry point: $76.87 (current); wait for pullback to $68-70 to initiate new positions | Target price (12 months): $82-$87 on 14-15x base case EPS of $5.75-$6.00 (10-15% upside) | Stop-loss: $67.00 (below the 52-week low; would signal a fundamental deterioration in underwriting or balance sheet confidence)

sources.marketData: Live quote data sourced via the DYOR HQ proprietary market data workflow using multiple market data inputs and validation checks. Fields include price, market capitalisation, 52-week range, P/E, EPS, and volume.
